categories.title: Start here
categories.description: Understand the product and choose the right first action.

categories.title: Studio
categories.description: Create a 30-second video or a finished infographic.

categories.title: Marketplace
categories.description: Post work, offer a Service, apply and complete paid work.

categories.title: Reputation and NexCard
categories.description: Build a public signal from X, then add the context X cannot know.

categories.title: Wallet and $NEX
categories.description: Add USDC, follow earnings and use the current $NEX utility.

categories.title: Resources and account
categories.description: Reuse approved material, control connections and stay notified.

categories.title: Help and policies
categories.description: Solve common problems and read the plain-language product rules.

articles.summary: NexMarkets brings media creation, paid work and professional reputation into one account.

sections.title: The product in one view
sections.body: Use Studio when you need a video or infographic. Use Marketplace when you want to hire, offer a Service or apply for work. Use Reputation to turn your public X activity into a NexCard and public profile.
points: The same account can create media, post work, apply, deliver, earn and add funds.
NexMind appears inside the two places that need conversation: video direction and Reputation enhancement.
Your Dashboard brings creations, applications, active delivery, balances and NexCard status together.

sections.title: What NexMarkets does not do
sections.body: NexMarkets does not publish, pay or release funds because a draft exists. A person confirms the important step first.
points: Studio waits for an approved task or video direction before production.
Marketplace waits for the Listing owner to fund and publish the work.
A Workroom waits for approval before payment is released.
Reputation changes are reviewed before they appear publicly.

sections.title: Choose the route that matches the job
sections.body: Begin with the result you need rather than trying to understand every part of the product first.
points: Create media: open Studio.
Hire or find paid work: open Marketplace.
Build or improve your public profile: open NexCard.
Add funds, check earnings or buy $NEX: open Wallet or $NEX.

articles.summary: Start without connecting everything. NexMarkets asks for an account, wallet or X only when the next action needs it.

sections.title: Create a video or infographic
sections.body: Open Studio and describe the finished piece. You can add source material and saved resources before payment.
points: Video: a 30-second creation shaped through a live NexMind direction session.
Infographic: a text-led task in 1920×1080 or 1080×1080.
Payment is requested after you have reviewed what Studio will make.

sections.title: Post or find work
sections.body: Open Marketplace to publish work, request a Service or apply for an opportunity.
points: A wallet is needed when you fund a Listing or receive payment.
An account is needed when you publish, request, apply or save work.
Browsing does not require a wallet.

sections.title: Build a NexCard
sections.body: Open NexCard to see the welcome page, then connect X when you are ready.
points: The base card uses public X identity and activity.
A wallet holding 50,000 $NEX can open profile enhancement with NexMind.
You choose what becomes public.

articles.summary: Your NexMarkets account, wallet, X account and Telegram connection serve different purposes and can be managed separately.

sections.title: NexMarkets account
sections.body: The account keeps your drafts, creations, Listings, applications, Workrooms, NexCard settings and notification history together.
points: Signing in does not connect a wallet automatically.
A wallet address is not used as your public name unless you choose it.
Signing out hides private workspace content but does not remove public profile pages.

sections.title: Wallet
sections.body: Connect a wallet when you need to add USDC, fund work, receive earnings, buy $NEX or check the 50,000 $NEX requirement.
points: A balance check cannot move funds.
The wallet asks you to approve each blockchain transaction.
Robinhood Chain uses ETH for gas.

sections.title: X and Telegram
sections.body: X is used only for the NexCard flow. Telegram is optional and is used for production updates worth returning for.
points: Connecting X does not connect a wallet.
Disconnecting Telegram does not stop Studio production.
Each connection can be reviewed from its relevant product area.

articles.summary: Dashboard is the account overview. My Work is the complete record of Marketplace activity.

sections.title: Use Dashboard for the current picture
sections.body: Dashboard shows your latest creation, posted work, applications, active delivery, balance, earnings and NexCard state.
points: Open a record to continue from the correct product area.
Money that needs approval appears with the related work.
The account overview changes as your own activity changes.

sections.title: Use My Work for the full Marketplace record
sections.body: My Work separates active work, applications, posted Listings and completed or declined records.
points: Every record is clickable, including declined applications.
Open an application to see the submitted date, current status and what happens next.
Open accepted work to enter its Workroom.

sections.title: Nothing important disappears
sections.body: Completed and declined records remain available so you can understand the outcome without searching through notifications.
points: A completed Workroom keeps delivery and approval history.
A declined application still links back to the original opportunity.
A finished Studio creation remains in the creations library.

articles.summary: Describe the finished video, supply the material it must respect and use the live NexMind session to settle the direction before production.

sections.title: Before you start
sections.body: Bring the clearest source you have: a website, announcement, product notes, screenshots, logo or reference material.
points: Videos are 30 seconds at launch.
You can use uploaded files and saved items from Your resources.
Do not include a claim that you cannot publish or support.

sections.title: Create the direction
sections.body: Open Studio, choose Video and describe what the film should achieve. Review the source summary, brand choices, destination and voice preference, then choose Speak with NexMind.
points: NexMind asks only for decisions that change the finished film.
You can interrupt, correct or type during the live session.
The production brief remains available from its compact brief icon.

sections.title: Approve and pay
sections.body: After the live session, review the final direction and payment before production begins.
points: The checkout currently shows 5 USDC, or 4 USDC when the connected wallet qualifies for the 50,000 $NEX rate.
Closing and returning to the paid session does not create a second charge.
Studio begins only after the wallet confirms payment.

articles.topic: Prepare the material for a video
articles.summary: The quality of the video depends on the clarity of the source, not the amount of material you upload.

sections.title: Use the strongest source first
sections.body: A live product page is useful when it contains current positioning and visual identity. Product notes are better when the public page is incomplete.
points: Add the logo in the highest-quality available format.
Add screenshots that must appear rather than expecting Studio to guess them.
Mark private files clearly and avoid uploading material you do not have permission to use.

sections.title: Choose from Your resources
sections.body: Open Your resources inside Studio, select the saved items you need and choose Use selected.
points: A selected item shows a checkmark before it is attached.
The selected count updates as you choose or remove items.
Video and infographic selections are kept separate.

sections.title: Keep the brief focused
sections.body: State the audience, the one idea that must land and the action the viewer should take. Leave decorative choices to the direction stage unless they are essential.
points: Useful: ‘A founder should understand how the workflow moves from brief to finished work.’
Too broad: ‘Make it exciting and premium.’
Call out words, claims or visuals that must not appear.

articles.summary: The live session is where the raw request becomes a production-ready direction. It is for video only.

sections.title: What happens in the session
sections.body: NexMind already has the source, assets and choices supplied in Studio. The conversation is used to settle the story, opening, emphasis, voice and final action.
points: The orb turns warm ivory while receiving your voice.
It moves outward in gold when NexMind speaks.
Speaking over NexMind stops its response and returns the session to listening.

sections.title: Controls on mobile
sections.body: The live view hides the app footer so no navigation sits over the conversation. The production brief opens from the small file icon in the header.
points: Pause stops the session without discarding the direction.
Mute prevents microphone input until you turn it back on.
Typed input remains available when speaking is inconvenient.

sections.title: When the direction is ready
sections.body: NexMind summarises the decisions. Review them rather than approving from memory.
points: Check the audience, message, duration, assets, voice and destination.
Correct any unsupported claim before payment.
Approval moves the direction to checkout; it does not publish the final video.

articles.topic: Video payment and returning to a session
articles.summary: The payment belongs to the approved video direction, not to every time you open the live session.

sections.title: Before confirmation
sections.body: The checkout shows the current Studio price, your available USDC and the balance after payment.
points: Review the connected wallet and network.
If the balance is too low, add USDC without losing the draft.
Do not approve a wallet request whose amount or network differs from the checkout.

sections.title: After payment
sections.body: The paid direction is stored with the creation. Leaving the page or returning to the same session does not create a second payment.
points: A materially different deliverable should be treated as a new creation.
A failed transaction leaves the direction unpaid and recoverable.
A confirmed transaction moves the creation into production.

sections.title: Price shown in the current product
sections.body: The current checkout uses a 5 USDC standard video price and a 4 USDC rate when the connected wallet holds at least 50,000 $NEX.
points: The qualifying balance is checked when the benefit is used.
The tokens remain in the wallet; there is no staking or lock.
The checkout is the final price shown before approval.

facts: No, for the same paid direction

articles.summary: Describe the visual, add the source material and approve a written task. There is no live voice session for an infographic.

sections.title: Add the request
sections.body: Choose Infographic and state what the finished visual should communicate. Add the source text, page, report, announcement or data that supports it.
points: Add the primary colour or use the supplied branding.
Upload logos, screenshots, photos, PDFs, documents, data files or references when they matter.

sections.title: Review the written task
sections.body: NexMind organises the information in text: the intended message, source, size, branding, required assets and any exclusions.
points: There is no orb, microphone or live call.
Edit the source or instruction before approval if the task is incomplete.
The written task is the reference Studio uses for production.

sections.title: Approve and create
sections.body: The infographic costs 0.10 USDC. Confirm the task and payment, then Studio begins immediately.
points: The charge is shown before the wallet request.
The finished image keeps the chosen 16:9 or square shape on desktop and mobile.
Production progress remains available after you leave Studio.

articles.keywords: image visual information graphic square landscape

articles.summary: Your resources is the reusable library for logos, screenshots, product material and approved references.

sections.title: Select rather than assume
sections.body: Choose Your resources inside the current video or infographic. Click an item to mark it selected, then choose Use selected.
points: The checkmark confirms selection; opening the library alone attaches nothing.
You can select several items in one visit.
Cancel closes the picker without changing the current creation.

sections.title: What happens after insertion
sections.body: Selected resources appear with the current creation and are included in the review step.
points: Video resources appear in the source and direction review.
Infographic resources appear under Relevant assets and in the written task.
Files uploaded directly in the same creation remain alongside saved resources.

sections.title: Keep the library usable
sections.body: Use clear names and replace outdated material rather than saving several indistinguishable copies.
points: Keep one current logo set.
Use dates or version names for changing product documents.
Remove future access to material that should no longer be used.

articles.summary: You can leave Studio after payment. The creation continues and returns to the account when a reviewable result is ready.

sections.title: Follow production
sections.body: Studio shows the current stage and the material available at that point. Do not treat an estimated completion time as a guaranteed delivery time.
points: A video moves from approved direction into production and review.
An infographic begins from the approved written task.
The creation remains visible from Dashboard and Studio.

sections.title: Notifications
sections.body: In-app notifications remain the main record. Telegram can be connected for production events that are worth returning for.
points: Useful events include a required decision, a first version and completion.
Routine internal steps do not need a message.
Opening a notification returns to the related creation.

sections.title: When production fails
sections.body: A failed stage should state what failed and whether you need to act. The approved task and payment record remain attached.
points: Do not pay again unless checkout clearly identifies a new charge.
Replace an unreadable or unsupported source when Studio asks for it.
Use support when the creation remains stuck after the recovery step.

articles.topic: Review, revise and download final work
articles.summary: The final-work page keeps the media at its original proportion and gives you only the actions relevant after delivery.

sections.title: Review the work
sections.body: The page shows the work title, creator and a short read-only description of what the video or image contains.
points: Video and image previews do not become tall phone-shaped cards on mobile.
A 16:9 item remains 16:9; square and portrait items keep their own proportions.
Public viewers do not see internal production stages or version history.

sections.title: Request a revision
sections.body: Use Request revision when the delivered work does not match the approved task or direction. State the exact change and point to the affected part.
points: A revision should refer to the agreed direction rather than introduce a new deliverable.
Large changes may require a new scope and price.
The creation record keeps the relationship between the request and the delivered result.

sections.title: Use the result
sections.body: Download the finished file or attach it to a Marketplace Listing when the creation supports paid work.
points: Video downloads retain the delivered aspect ratio.
Infographic exports use the approved 1920×1080 or 1080×1080 size.
Do not publish private client material without permission.

articles.summary: Choose the type from the way the work will be bought and completed—not from how impressive the label sounds.

sections.title: Task
sections.body: Use a Task for one defined result with a clear delivery and approval condition.
points: Example: produce one product explainer by Friday.
A Task can pay one person or several people when the same outcome is being commissioned more than once.
Set the offer, timing and approval condition before publication.

sections.title: Service
sections.body: A Service is a fixed offer published by the provider. Buyers request it after reviewing what is included, what they must supply, the price and delivery time.
points: Use Request when you want to buy the listed Service.
Do not post a duplicate Task unless you need a different scope.
The provider's availability applies to the offer shown.

sections.title: Role, Campaign and Direct Hire
sections.body: Use a Role for ongoing responsibility, a Campaign for several approved contributors and Direct Hire for a private offer to one selected person or project.
points: A Campaign should state the number of available places and payment per contributor.
A Role should state expected commitment and payment period.
Direct Hire is visible only to the invited recipient.

articles.summary: Use the Post Work flow to define the result, who should do it, the offer and the exact condition for release.

sections.title: Describe the work
sections.body: Choose the Listing type, write a direct title and describe the finished result. Add deliverables and useful skills.
points: Describe what must be handed over, not every activity the worker may perform.
Avoid titles such as ‘Need help’ or ‘Looking for expert.’
Attach only material an applicant needs before selection.

sections.title: Choose one person
sections.body: In People, select One person. Choose whether applications are public, limited to selected invitations or invite-only.
points: State who is likely to succeed without excluding qualified people through vague prestige requirements.
Use private visibility when the work or source material is sensitive.
The selected person enters the Workroom after acceptance.

sections.title: Set the offer
sections.body: Enter the amount paid to that person, the delivery period, application deadline, release condition and approval criteria.
points: The total funding equals the per-person offer for a one-person Listing.
The full amount must be available before publication.
Review the complete Listing before securing funds.

articles.summary: A group Listing pays several people under one clearly stated opportunity.

sections.title: Set the number of places
sections.body: Choose Group and enter the number of people who can be selected.
points: Use a Campaign when several contributors are part of one coordinated public effort.
Use a group Task when the same defined result is being commissioned from several people.
Do not call unpaid participation a paid place.

sections.title: Set payment per person
sections.body: Enter what each selected person receives. NexMarkets calculates the full amount that must be secured.
points: Four places at 250 USDC each require 1,000 USDC.
Changing the number of places changes the total immediately.
The review screen shows both the per-person offer and total commitment.

sections.title: Make selection fair
sections.body: State whether applicants are reviewed together, selected as places fill or invited directly.
points: Give every selected contributor the same stated terms unless the Listing clearly defines tiers.
Do not promise more places than the funded total supports.
Each accepted contributor receives their own delivery and payment record.

articles.topic: Set the offer, funding and approval condition
articles.summary: A clear Listing tells both sides what is being paid, when delivery is due and what counts as accepted work.

sections.title: Offer
sections.body: Set the amount paid to each person. For a group, the review screen also shows the full commitment.
points: Use USDC amounts that match the scope and number of people.
Do not hide required unpaid work in the application stage.
A changed offer should be visible before anyone accepts.

sections.title: Approval condition
sections.body: Write an observable condition for release. ‘When satisfied’ is not enough.
points: Good: final 1920×1080 file, editable source and two supplied data corrections included.
Good: video matches the approved 30-second script and supplied brand assets.
Avoid criteria that appear only after delivery.

sections.title: Securing funds
sections.body: NexMarkets checks the total against the account balance before publication and secures the required amount for the Listing.
points: An unfunded draft remains a draft.
A failed wallet transaction does not erase the Listing.
Payment releases from the Workroom under the condition shown before work began.

articles.summary: A Service is bought from the provider's published offer rather than created through the Post Work form.

sections.title: Read the offer first
sections.body: Open View service to see the result, included deliverables, buyer inputs, delivery time, fixed price and current availability.
points: Confirm that your request fits the stated scope.
Check what you must supply before the provider can begin.
Ask for a separate scope when the request materially exceeds the offer.

sections.title: Send the request
sections.body: Choose Request, add the material the provider needs and review the delivery and approval terms.
points: A request does not rewrite the provider's public Service.
Sensitive material should be shared after the correct private record opens.
The provider can accept or decline the request.

sections.title: After acceptance
sections.body: An accepted Service request opens a Workroom with the agreed scope, files, delivery and payment condition.
points: Use the Workroom for changes and delivery.
Approve the result only after checking the agreed inclusions.
The completed record stays in My Work.

articles.summary: A useful Service card makes the buyer understand the result before opening the full offer.

sections.title: Define the offer
sections.body: Name the outcome, not only your skill. State what the buyer receives and what must be supplied.
points: Better: ‘Turn a product brief into a launch-ready 30-second script.’
Weaker: ‘Professional copywriting service.’
Use a fixed price and delivery time that apply to the scope shown.

sections.title: Set boundaries
sections.body: State what is included, what is not included and how revisions are handled.
points: Avoid open-ended promises such as unlimited changes.
Identify file formats or handover material.
Pause availability when you cannot take another request.

sections.title: Respond to requests
sections.body: Review the buyer's supplied material before accepting. If the request exceeds the Service, propose a new Listing or decline it clearly.
points: Acceptance creates the Workroom.
Changes after acceptance belong in the Workroom record.
Completed Service work contributes to your Marketplace history.

articles.summary: Open a Listing to understand the result, offer, timing and approval condition before you apply.

sections.title: Choose work that fits
sections.body: Use filters and the full Listing details rather than applying from a title alone.
points: Check whether the Listing is funded.
Check the delivery period and application deadline.
Read the requested capabilities and any required answer.

sections.title: Prepare the application
sections.body: Answer the project questions directly and choose work that shows the relevant ability.
points: Do not claim a role you did not perform.
Use approved NexCard fields where they strengthen the application.
A short specific application is stronger than a generic introduction.

sections.title: Follow the result
sections.body: Your application appears in My Work and remains clickable after submission.
points: Applied means the owner has not selected yet.
Shortlisted means you remain under consideration.
Declined remains visible with the original Listing and outcome.

articles.summary: Compare applicants against the actual work rather than choosing from follower count or a polished headline alone.

sections.title: Open every relevant application
sections.body: Review the applicant's answer, selected work, approved NexCard fields and availability.
points: A match explanation should state why the work fits.
Open the public profile when you need more context.
Do not treat inferred reputation as client-approved Marketplace work.

sections.title: Shortlist or select
sections.body: Use shortlist when you need to compare a smaller group. Select only the number of funded places in the Listing.
points: For group work, keep the remaining place count visible.
Do not ask shortlisted applicants to complete the paid deliverable for free.
Selection should preserve the offer and approval terms they applied to.

sections.title: What selection creates
sections.body: The accepted work moves into a Workroom. Applicants who are not selected retain the outcome in My Work.
points: The selected person receives the approved scope and deadline.
Secured funds remain tied to the work.
A new scope needs a clear change rather than an informal message.

articles.summary: My Work is the record of what you posted, applied for, accepted, delivered or completed.

sections.title: Applications
sections.body: Applied, Shortlisted and Declined records remain clickable.
points: Applied: waiting for a decision.
Shortlisted: still under consideration.
Declined: not selected; the record remains available.

sections.title: Active work
sections.body: Accepted and In delivery records open the related Workroom.
points: Awaiting your delivery means the next action is yours.
Awaiting review means the other side has a submitted result to assess.
Revision requested means the Workroom should state the exact change.

sections.title: Finished records
sections.body: Completed, Cancelled and Disputed work remain in Past with the relevant outcome.
points: Completed retains delivery and approval history.
Cancelled should state whether any funds were returned or released.
Disputed retains the evidence being reviewed.

articles.summary: A Workroom is the shared record that opens after work is accepted or a Service request is approved.

sections.title: Use the approved scope
sections.body: The Workroom keeps the result, deliverables, timing, participants and release condition in one place.
points: Share working files and decisions inside the Workroom.
Record a scope change before doing the additional work.
Keep private material inside the authorised workspace.

sections.title: Submit delivery
sections.body: The person doing the work submits the final material with a short note that identifies what is included.
points: Use the correct file or link rather than a temporary preview that will expire.
Call out any agreed limitation.
Submission changes the record to awaiting review.

sections.title: Approve, revise or dispute
sections.body: Approve when the result meets the stated condition. Request a revision when a specific agreed item is missing. Use dispute only when the parties cannot resolve the record directly.
points: Approval releases payment under the shown condition.
A revision request should be tied to the scope.
A dispute pauses release while the relevant record is reviewed.

articles.summary: These Listing types are useful when a one-off public Task is not the right shape.

sections.title: Role
sections.body: Use a Role for ongoing responsibility. State the expected commitment, payment period, decision rights and review point.
points: Avoid presenting full-time expectations as a small one-off Task.
State whether the role is remote and whether time-zone overlap matters.
Explain how either side can end the arrangement.

sections.title: Campaign
sections.body: Use a Campaign for several contributors working under one public objective.
points: State the number of paid places and amount per person.
Explain whether every approved contribution receives the same deliverable brief.
Do not hide selection criteria or required disclosure.

sections.title: Direct Hire
sections.body: Use Direct Hire when you already know who you want to invite. The offer is private to the recipient.
points: Include the product, desired result, timing and offer.
The recipient can accept, decline or ask for clarification.
Acceptance opens the Workroom under the agreed terms.

articles.topic: Start a NexCard and connect X
articles.summary: The Reputation tab always begins with a clear welcome page until the current user connects their own X account.

sections.title: What you see first
sections.body: The page explains that NexCard turns public X activity into a shareable work-and-network signal. It does not reveal a previous user's card or saved profile details.
points: Being signed in or wallet-connected does not skip the X welcome step.
Signed-out visitors see the welcome page, not private Reputation controls.
The public profile remains a separate public page.

sections.title: Connect X
sections.body: Choose Connect X to authorise the public account used for the base NexCard.
points: The connection should identify the current X account before analysis begins.
Disconnecting X stops future refreshes but does not silently delete published material.
Use the account you want represented publicly.

sections.title: What happens next
sections.body: NexMarkets analyses the available public identity and recent activity, then reveals the base NexCard for review.
points: No wallet is required for the base card.
Nothing from the analysis becomes a client-approved Marketplace record.
You can enhance the profile later when eligible.

articles.summary: X supplies public identity and public activity. It does not supply your full professional story.

sections.title: Identity fields
sections.body: NexMarkets can use the profile image, display name, handle, account creation date and user-entered location returned for the connected account.
points: An X location is user-entered and should not be treated as verified.
Remote availability is not inferred from a location field.
Initials are used only when a profile image is unavailable.

sections.title: Public activity
sections.body: The base analysis can use recent public posts, public links, timestamps and available public metrics to describe reach, conversation, consistency, momentum and recurring topics.
points: The private screen may say how many data points were checked.
A topic is a public-activity pattern, not a confirmed job title.
A Marketplace delivery is added from NexMarkets records, not from X.

sections.title: What is not requested
sections.body: The NexCard flow does not need direct messages, drafts, bookmarks or private lists.
points: A polished professional line is not presented as X data.
Availability and preferred work require the user's confirmation.
The user reviews what will be public.

articles.keywords: posts data points profile location public metrics

articles.summary: The base card is a concise visual account of public activity before the user adds professional context.

sections.title: What the card shows
sections.body: The card can show identity, recent reach, conversation quality, posting consistency, momentum and recurring topics.
points: It should not claim a role that has not been confirmed.
It should not show a public contract amount.
It should remain readable at the 16:9 share size.

sections.title: What the card is for
sections.body: The card introduces the person. The public profile holds the deeper explanation, selected work and hiring action.
points: Download the card as a shareable image.
Copy the canonical public profile link separately.
The card and profile should not repeat the same paragraph.

sections.title: Before enhancement
sections.body: The base card remains cautious because X cannot know the person's exact role, desired work, availability or contribution to every project.
points: Keep the base card when it already serves the user's needs.
Enhancement is optional.
The base card can be refreshed as public activity changes.

articles.summary: Profile enhancement adds the human context that cannot be established from public posts alone.

sections.title: Access requirement
sections.body: The connected wallet must hold at least 50,000 $NEX. The tokens stay in the wallet; there is no staking or lock.
points: A qualifying wallet enters the existing live Reputation session.
An underqualified wallet sees the current holding, required holding and amount still needed.
Buy $NEX opens the dedicated purchase route.

sections.title: What the conversation covers
sections.body: NexMind asks targeted questions about role, work direction, availability, location preference, desired opportunities and contribution to completed work.
points: The conversation should correct an overstatement rather than polish it.
A project contribution is stored separately from the deliverable format.
Private details can remain private.

sections.title: Nothing changes yet
sections.body: Completing the conversation creates proposed profile details. It does not publish them automatically.
points: The user sees a review screen next.
Each proposed field can be edited, removed or kept private.
The card and public profile update only after Publish changes.

articles.summary: The review screen is where the user decides what NexMind understood correctly and what should never appear publicly.

sections.title: Review each field
sections.body: Check the public role, work description, preferred work, availability, location preference and project contribution separately.
points: Edit wording that sounds unlike the person.
Remove an unsupported capability.
Keep sensitive context private without discarding it from private matching.

sections.title: Understand the source
sections.body: The private Reputation view should distinguish X-derived activity, user-confirmed context and approved Marketplace work.
points: A conversation does not turn an outside project into Marketplace-approved work.
Client approval comes only from the related Workroom record.
Payment amounts remain private by default.

sections.title: Publish changes
sections.body: Choose Publish changes only after the card and profile preview read correctly.
points: The enhanced card gains the confirmed professional line and availability chosen for public display.
The public profile gains the fuller first-person explanation.
Matching can use approved private preferences without exposing them publicly.

articles.summary: The public profile expands the NexCard through real work, clear contribution and a direct invitation to collaborate.

sections.title: Public profile
sections.body: The public page uses its own simple header and does not expose private account navigation or owner-only controls.
points: The hero identifies the person, work direction and availability.
Invite to work is the primary public action.
The canonical link uses the public /u/username route.

sections.title: Selected work
sections.body: Selected work is reserved for completed work approved through NexMarkets Marketplace.
points: Show the project name, the person's contribution and the outcome.
Use the real delivered preview when permission allows.
Do not show the contract amount by default.

sections.title: Outside work
sections.body: Work added by the owner from outside NexMarkets should be labelled separately so it is not confused with a Marketplace-approved delivery.
points: The owner remains responsible for the accuracy of outside work.
A public source can support the claim but is not client approval.
Remove work that can no longer be shown publicly.

articles.summary: Opportunity matching becomes more useful after the user confirms the work they want and the availability that should inform matches.

sections.title: How matching improves
sections.body: Before enhancement, matching can use broad public topics and account activity. After enhancement it can also use confirmed skills, preferred work, availability and Marketplace delivery history.
points: A match should explain the evidence behind the fit.
A high number alone is not a reason to recommend work.
The Listing owner still decides who is selected.

sections.title: Public and private fields
sections.body: A field can help private matching without being displayed on the public profile.
points: Availability may be public, private for matching or removed.
Private project information should not become profile copy.
Review public visibility whenever the work changes.

sections.title: Refresh, pause or remove
sections.body: Refresh X when public activity needs updating. Pause the public page when it should not be visible. Remove the card only when the user understands what public links will stop working.
points: Pausing is faster than deleting the account.
Completed Marketplace records remain part of the private account history.
Reconnect X before requesting a fresh public-activity analysis.

articles.topic: Connect a wallet and add USDC
articles.summary: USDC is used for Studio creation and Marketplace work. Connect the wallet only when a paid action or balance check needs it.

sections.title: Connect the wallet
sections.body: Choose the wallet action from checkout, Wallet or $NEX. Confirm the address and Robinhood Chain before continuing.
points: Robinhood Chain mainnet uses Chain ID 4663.
ETH is used for gas.
A wallet connection allows balance checks but cannot move funds by itself.

sections.title: Add USDC
sections.body: Open Wallet and choose Add funds. Send or bridge USDC using the supported route shown in the product.
points: Confirm the token contract and network before sending.
Do not send funds to an address provided in an unsolicited message.
Wait for the transaction to confirm before funding work.

sections.title: Use the balance
sections.body: Studio checkout and Marketplace publication show the required amount and balance after payment.
points: A low balance does not remove the draft.
USDC reserved for active work is shown separately from available funds.
Earnings become available according to the Workroom release record.

articles.topic: Available balance, secured funds and earnings
articles.summary: The Wallet separates money you can use now from money committed to work or waiting to be released.

sections.title: Available balance
sections.body: Available USDC can be used for Studio or to fund a new Listing.
points: Adding funds increases available balance after confirmation.
Funding a Listing moves the required amount into its secured state.
A pending transaction should not be counted twice.

sections.title: Secured funds
sections.body: Secured funds belong to a named Listing or Workroom and are not available for unrelated spending.
points: The worker can see that the work is funded.
The owner can see the total commitment.
A group Listing secures the offer for all published places.

sections.title: Earnings and pending release
sections.body: Earnings come from completed Marketplace work. Pending release means delivery exists but the approval condition has not yet released the funds.
points: Open the related Workroom for the next action.
Do not treat pending release as available balance.
Completed releases remain in transaction history.

articles.topic: Payment release, refunds and failed transactions
articles.summary: Every payment state should remain connected to the creation, Listing or Workroom that caused it.

sections.title: Release
sections.body: The hiring side releases Marketplace payment by approving the delivered work under the stated condition.
points: Review the actual submission before approval.
A release should show the recipient and amount.
Blockchain confirmation may take longer than the interface action.

sections.title: Refund or cancellation
sections.body: A refund depends on the state of the work and the terms visible in its record.
points: An unfunded draft needs no refund.
A cancelled funded Listing should state what amount returns and what remains committed.
Do not resolve an active dispute by moving the conversation outside the Workroom.

sections.title: Failed or pending transaction
sections.body: If the wallet request fails or remains pending, keep the underlying draft and use the transaction record to decide the next step.
points: Do not repeat the transaction until you know the first request failed.
Check the wallet and block explorer when the app status is uncertain.
Never share a recovery phrase with support.

facts: Keep the draft and check transaction state

articles.summary: $NEX is the utility token of NexMarkets. Its current in-product use is holder access to NexMind profile enhancement.

sections.title: Current utility
sections.body: A connected wallet holding at least 50,000 $NEX can enhance the NexCard and public profile with NexMind.
points: The base NexCard remains available without the token threshold.
The tokens remain in the wallet.
No staking or locking is required for this access.

sections.title: What enhancement changes
sections.body: The session helps the user confirm role, availability, preferred work and project contribution, followed by a review before publication.
points: Holding $NEX does not buy a reputation score.
It does not guarantee Marketplace work.
Public changes still require the user's approval.

sections.title: Balance check
sections.body: Eligibility is checked from the connected wallet when the user requests enhancement.
points: Falling below the threshold does not erase the base card or completed work.
Use the $NEX page to check balance and token information.
Verify the official contract before buying.

articles.summary: Use the Buy $NEX action on the $NEX page and verify the official NexMarkets token before approving the trade.

sections.title: Start from NexMarkets
sections.body: Open $NEX, connect the wallet that will hold the token and choose Buy $NEX.
points: The purchase route opens the official market used by NexMarkets.
Search results or unsolicited links are not a substitute for the official route.
The app should never ask you to send tokens to unlock access.

sections.title: Verify before approval
sections.body: Confirm the token name, network and official contract address published on the NexMarkets page.
points: Use Robinhood Chain, not a similarly named network.
Review the amount received and price impact.
Keep enough ETH for gas.

sections.title: Check access
sections.body: Return to $NEX or Reputation and refresh the connected-wallet balance.
points: The wallet must hold at least 50,000 $NEX for profile enhancement.
A pending purchase may not appear until the transaction confirms.
The tokens stay under the wallet owner's control.

articles.topic: Live price, chart and token details
articles.summary: The $NEX page is the source for the current market feed, connected-wallet position and verified token details.

sections.title: Market panel
sections.body: When the verified market feed is available, the page shows current price, 24-hour movement and an interactive chart.
points: Market data can move quickly and may differ slightly across venues.
A missing feed should not be replaced with a simulated price.
Use the chart as market information, not a promise of future value.

sections.title: Token details
sections.body: The page lists Robinhood Chain, Chain ID 4663, ETH as the gas token, the launch platform and the official contract when configured.
points: Copy the contract from the NexMarkets page rather than from a reply or direct message.
Check the block explorer for the token and transaction.
A ticker alone is not enough to identify a token.

sections.title: Wallet position
sections.body: The connected-wallet panel shows the detected $NEX balance and whether the holder-access threshold is met.
points: The page reads the public balance; it does not take custody.
Refresh after a confirmed purchase or transfer.
Use Reputation to begin enhancement after access is detected.

articles.summary: Your resources is the reusable library for material you expect to use again across Studio and active work.

sections.title: What belongs here
sections.body: Save current logos, product screenshots, approved product facts, reference documents and other reusable material.
points: Use clear names that explain the file without opening it.
Keep sensitive client material in the correct restricted workspace.
Do not use the library as a dumping ground for every draft.

sections.title: Using a resource
sections.body: Open the picker from Studio, choose one or more items and confirm with Use selected.
points: A checkmark means the item is selected.
The selected count confirms how many items will be inserted.
Closing or cancelling does not attach the items.

sections.title: Replace outdated material
sections.body: Upload the replacement, give it a clear current name and remove future access to the obsolete item when appropriate.
points: Completed work keeps its original record.
A removed resource should not disappear from historical payment or delivery evidence.
Check active drafts before deleting a source they still need.

articles.summary: In-app notifications are the account record. Telegram is an optional way to receive selected production updates.

sections.title: What creates a useful notification
sections.body: A message should tell you what changed and take you back to the exact record.
points: A Studio version is ready.
An application or invitation changed state.
A delivery needs review or a payment was released.

sections.title: Telegram
sections.body: Connect Telegram when you want important Studio production events outside the app.
points: Disconnecting Telegram does not cancel production.
Routine internal rendering steps should not create noise.
The in-app notice remains available if the external message is missed.

sections.title: Act from the record
sections.body: Open the notification rather than acting from a summary alone.
points: Review a version in Studio.
Review delivery in the Workroom.
Review a wallet release from the related payment record.

articles.summary: Manage X, wallet and notification connections independently and keep public profile choices separate from private workspace data.

sections.title: Connections
sections.body: Review X from Reputation, wallet from Wallet and Telegram from notification settings.
points: Removing one connection should not remove unrelated account content.
Use the current account rather than assuming a previously connected identity.
Re-authorise when a provider permission expires.

sections.title: Public profile choices
sections.body: Only fields approved for public display belong on the NexCard and public profile.
points: Private availability can still inform matching when the user allows it.
Your resources do not become public because they were used in Studio.
Payment amounts remain private unless a user and counterparty deliberately make them public.

sections.title: Signing out and deletion
sections.body: Signing out ends access on the current device. Account deletion may require active work and secured payments to be resolved first.
points: Pause a public NexCard when immediate public removal is needed.
Export account data before deletion when required.
Never share a wallet recovery phrase with NexMarkets support.

articles.summary: NexMarkets is designed for phones without forcing desktop layouts into narrow screens.

sections.title: Mobile navigation
sections.body: The phone footer keeps Dashboard, Studio, $NEX, Market and NexCard within reach. Immersive live video direction hides the footer until the session ends.
points: Sheets open from the bottom where appropriate.
Tap outside an open popup to close it.
Important actions remain above the safe area.

sections.title: Media proportions
sections.body: Studio previews fit the phone without changing the shape of the work.
points: Square stays square.
Portrait stays portrait.

sections.title: Accessibility controls
sections.body: Use Settings to increase text, strengthen contrast or reduce motion.
points: Reduced motion removes non-essential route and orb movement.
Larger text should not shrink touch targets.
Keyboard focus and visible labels remain part of desktop use.

articles.summary: Start from the record that failed. NexMarkets should preserve the draft, source or payment context needed to recover.

sections.title: A button does nothing
sections.body: Confirm that the page is fully loaded, then close any open sheet or popup and try once. If the action still fails, keep the record open and note the exact button.
points: Do not repeatedly approve wallet requests.
Refresh only after the draft has been saved.
Check whether the action requires sign-in, X or wallet access.

sections.title: A payment is pending
sections.body: Check the wallet and Robinhood Chain transaction before sending another payment.
points: A pending transaction can take time to confirm.
A failed transaction should leave the creation or Listing draft intact.
Use the transaction hash when contacting support.

sections.title: A creation or Workroom is stuck
sections.body: Open the related record from Dashboard, Studio or My Work and read the current state before restarting anything.
points: Replace an unusable source when Studio identifies it.
Respond to an open decision in the Workroom.
Contact support when no recovery action is shown.

articles.summary: Send enough information to identify the problem without sharing wallet secrets or unrelated private material.

sections.title: Include
sections.body: State what you were doing, what you expected, what happened and the relevant creation, Listing, Workroom or transaction reference.
points: Include the time and device when the issue is intermittent.
Attach a screenshot only when it does not expose private keys or confidential content.
Keep replies in the same support thread.

sections.title: Urgent issues
sections.body: Clearly identify unauthorised account access, a suspected fake token route, an unsafe Listing or a payment that may release incorrectly.
points: Use the wallet provider's security controls immediately for wallet compromise.
Report a misleading Listing from its record when possible.
Do not wait for support before securing a compromised external account.

sections.title: What support will never ask for
sections.body: NexMarkets support should never request a seed phrase, private key or direct token transfer.
points: A legitimate balance check happens through the connected wallet.
A legitimate purchase route begins from the $NEX page.
A legitimate payment request identifies the related work.

articles.summary: These plain-language terms explain the basic agreement behind using NexMarkets. Product-specific details shown at checkout or inside a Workroom also apply to that action.

sections.title: Use your own account and material
sections.body: Provide accurate account information and use only material, wallets and connected accounts you are allowed to use.
points: Do not upload stolen, misleading or unlawful material.
Do not impersonate another person, project or agent.
Keep your login and wallet security under your control.

sections.title: You approve important actions
sections.body: NexMarkets provides tools for creation, reputation and paid work. You remain responsible for the content you publish, the claims you approve and the wallet transactions you sign.
points: Drafts do not count as approval.
Marketplace scope and payment conditions shown before acceptance form part of the work record.
Blockchain transactions may not be reversible after confirmation.

sections.title: Marketplace conduct
sections.body: Describe work honestly, pay under the agreed condition and deliver what was accepted.
points: Do not ask applicants to complete the paid deliverable for free.
Do not move a dispute outside the Workroom to hide the record.
NexMarkets may restrict accounts used for fraud, abuse or repeated non-delivery.

sections.title: Availability and changes
sections.body: Features, prices and token utility may change as the product develops. Material changes should be dated and shown before they affect a paid or public action.
points: Current checkout controls the amount you approve.
Current Workroom terms control the named piece of work.
Stop using the product if you do not agree with an updated term.

articles.summary: NexMarkets uses the information needed to run the feature you choose and keeps public-profile choices separate from private account material.

sections.title: Information you provide
sections.body: This can include account details, uploaded resources, Studio instructions, Marketplace messages, applications, delivery files and support requests.
points: Do not upload information you do not need for the task.
Workspace and Workroom access controls apply to shared material.
Public profile fields are chosen separately.

sections.title: Connected services
sections.body: X provides the public identity and activity authorised for the NexCard flow. A wallet provides its public address, relevant balances and transactions. Telegram receives only the notifications the user enables.
points: A wallet balance check cannot move funds.
X direct messages, drafts, bookmarks and private lists are not needed for the base NexCard.
Disconnect a service from the relevant settings when it is no longer needed.

sections.title: How information is used
sections.body: Information is used to save work, produce approved creations, operate Marketplace records, explain matches, process notifications, support payments, prevent abuse and recover failed actions.
points: Private resources are not public by default.
Approved private matching fields need not appear on the public profile.
Service providers should receive only the information needed for their function.

sections.title: Your controls
sections.body: Use Settings and the relevant product page to update connections, public fields, notifications and account data.
points: Pause or remove the public NexCard from Reputation.
Request account export or deletion from Settings.
Some payment and completed-work records may need to remain for security, disputes or legal obligations.
